Specific English or History postings will appear between now and August; candidates should submit to each district’s secondary pool and monitor individual vacancy alerts. Salary ranges are typically published only when a specific school-site vacancy is posted.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the 2026-27 mailing list. Auditions for all voice parts are held each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Seattle Choral Company will audition tenors and basses on Tuesday, September 2, 2025, 5:00 pm at Phinney Ridge Lutheran Church; repertoire includes “Christmas in Latvia,” Bach & Handel masterworks, and Dett/Hailstork/Esmail’s “Chariot Jubilee.” Choral Arts Northwest’s 32nd season runs Oct 2025–May 2026 with concerts in October, December, March, and May; no specific audition dates posted yet. Pacific Chorale opens registration May 1 at 10 AM for its August 14–16 Choral Festival—no audition required to sing Duruflé’s Requiem with hundreds of voices.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library offers Toddler Storytime with Cami on Thursdays: May 7 & May 28, 9:00–9:45 am at 650 Main St. Lynnwood Library hosts Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Monday, May 4, 9:30–10:00 am at 19200 44th Ave W. Both Sno-Isle branches also list Baby Storytime (May 1 & May 12) and Preschool Storytime (May 6) for under-5s.

global_news.findings: President Trump declared the Iran war "terminated" in a letter to Congress on May 1, claiming the 60-day War Powers Resolution deadline doesn't apply due to an ongoing ceasefire. This came as Iran submitted a new peace proposal through Pakistani mediators, which Trump promptly rejected as insufficient. The administration argues the ceasefire effectively paused the legal clock, though legal experts dispute this interpretation.

The Pentagon announced major AI partnerships with Google, OpenAI, Amazon, Microsoft, SpaceX, Oracle, Nvidia and Reflection to transform the US military into an "AI-first fighting force." These contracts will deploy AI for "lawful operational use" across defense operations. Notably absent is Anthropic, which refused similar contracts over ethical concerns and is now suing the government after being labeled a "supply chain risk."

The US fast-tracked $8 billion in arms sales to Middle East allies as tensions with Iran remain high. Iran's parliament is preparing legislation to restrict shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, requiring vessels from "hostile countries" to pay reparations for passage. A senior Iranian military official warned renewed conflict is possible after Trump rejected Tehran's latest peace proposal.

President Trump signed sweeping new sanctions against Cuba targeting officials in energy, defense, financial and security sectors, along with those accused of human rights abuses. Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez condemned the measures as "illegal and abusive" violations of the UN Charter. Trump told supporters the US would be "taking over" Cuba "almost immediately" after finishing with Iran, prompting mass protests in Havana.

Diplomatic efforts to end the Iran war hang in the balance as President Trump publicly stated he's "not happy" with Iran's latest proposal and sees no early resolution. The US has requested a two-month ceasefire extension, but Iran insists issues must be resolved within 30 days. Both sides continue military preparations while maintaining the fragile ceasefire.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica investigation reveals how President Trump's administration has systematically dismantled federal election safeguards ahead of the 2026 midterms. The report documents at least 75 career staff departures across key agencies, replaced by two dozen appointees from the election denial movement—ten of whom previously attempted to overturn the 2020 election. The piece reconstructs a previously unreported December 2020 meeting where Attorney General William Barr convened cybersecurity experts to investigate Trump's claims about hacked voting machines in Michigan. After experts de [TRUNCATED]

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Francine Prose's review of two contemporary novels—Lee Clay Johnson's "Bloodline" and Carl Hiaasen's "Fever Beach"—examines how fiction can address our fractured political moment without sacrificing narrative pleasure. Both novels, she argues, channel the "grotesque and infuriating nature of contemporary public life" through darkly comic lenses that initially mask their serious reckoning with escalating violence, corruption, and class resentment. Johnson's "Bloodline" follows a former used car salesman who deliberately saws off his hand in an insurance scam, rebranding himself "Wins-a-ton" in  [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy's essay reframes Marcel Proust's 1905 defense of reading as a radical act of personal freedom rather than moral improvement. Drawing on Proust's preface to John Ruskin's "Sesame and Lilies," the piece argues that literature's value lies not in making us "better people" but in preserving "indelible impressions" that keep past sensations alive and make time tangible. Champy traces Proust's disagreement with Ruskin's moralizing approach—where reading serves collective spiritual betterment—to his alternative vision of books as creating unique "forms of communication" that bypass socia [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova's meditation on Oliver Sacks explores how neurological disorders reveal the creative nature of perception itself. Drawing from Sacks's posthumous collection "The River of Consciousness," the piece examines patients whose brain lesions create "standstills of consciousness"—moments where time freezes while the world continues. Popova synthesizes Sacks's argument that perception isn't passive reception but an active "creative act that marshals all that we are and reflects us back to ourselves." The essay connects neurological case studies to broader philosophical questions about cons [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Two fresh contributions to the triadic-ontology program appeared this week. First, Matteo Raffaelli’s ‘Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk’ (Triertium, 21 Feb 2026) extends his relational-gunk model to quantum domains, arguing that perichoretic relations are non-well-founded sets whose membership graphs exhibit triadic cycles impossible in classical mereology. Raffaelli shows that when gunk—objects lacking ultimate parts—is modeled within a triadic logic, the usual dichotomy between substance and relation dissolves: every subsistence is an event of relating, and every relation is itself a subsistent gunk-particle. Second, the Karl Alber series released volume 4, ‘Studies on Triadic Ontology: Quantum Perichoresis and Non-linear Dynamics’ (April 2026), edited by Donald MacKenzie. The lead essay, Ian Penman’s ‘I’m Just a Sound’, proposes that quantum entanglement is the physical signature of triadic perichoresis: three qubits locked in a non-se [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three major advances in autonomous research systems emerged this quarter. DOVA (Deep Orchestrated Versatile Agent) introduces deliberation-first orchestration where meta-reasoning precedes tool invocation, cutting inference costs 40-60% on simple tasks while preserving deep reasoning for complex queries. Paper Circle offers an open-source multi-agent framework that turns papers into structured knowledge graphs, enabling graph-aware Q&A and reproducible JSON/CSV/BibTeX outputs at every step. Mimosa pushes workflow evolution further by auto-synthesizing task-specific multi-agent topologies and iteratively refining them via experimental feedback, achieving 43.1% success on ScienceAgentBench. Meanwhile, RASPRef shows how retrieval-augmented prompt refinement can boost reasoning models without human labels, while new prompting guides reveal that o3, Claude 4.6, and Gemini 2.5 Pro now reward clarity over complexity—shifting the prompt engineer’s role from eliciting reasoning to directing pre [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: The House Oversight Committee’s Task Force on Declassification, chaired by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, held hearings in September 2025 focused on UAP transparency and whistleblower protection. Witnesses—mostly military pilots—described encounters with unexplained craft, including a 2021 Langley AFB sighting of a plasma-like triangle and Vandenberg incidents where personnel were told to stay quiet. Members aired newly released drone footage showing a missile deflected by an orb off Yemen. The Task Force continues pressing the Pentagon and AARO for withheld videos and clearer disclosure, framing UAPs as a national-security issue demanding public accountability.

newyorker.title: Medallions, Movement, and Mamdani at MOMA PS1
newyorker.dek: The cab-driving Elcharfa brothers, Bilal and Salah, star in a new piece by the artist Kenneth Tam that showcases the hardships of their jobs, Emma Allen writes.
newyorker.text:
On Election Night, Mayor Zohran Mamdani proclaimed, from a stage, “My brother, we are in City Hall now!” The remark was directed at Richard Chow, a cabdriver from Myanmar whom Mamdani had joined in 2021 on a fifteen-day hunger strike, to demand relief for the taxi-medallion debt crisis. The crisis, in brief: the N.Y.C. Taxi and Limousine Commission sells permits (“medallions”) to operate yellow cabs; the medallion—till recently a near-million-dollar investment—had its bottom drop out, owing to predatory lending and the deregulation of rideshare services. The body of Chow’s brother, Kenny, who’d accrued enormous medallion-related debt, was found in the East River in 2018. Chow would later chauffeur Mamdani to Gracie Mansion.
The other day, the artist Kenneth Tam, forty-four, was in Queens, admiring his contribution to MOMA PS1’s “Greater New York” survey of art with ties to the metropolitan area. Title: “I’m Staying Hopeful and Strong (for Bilal and Salah).” A projected video showed the sixtysomething Lebanese taxi-driver brothers Bilal and Salaheldin Elcharfa performing interpretive movements in an empty gymnasium while intoning words they wrote about the crisis (“no retirement no retirement no retirement / scared scared scared”). Also, on the floor of the gallery: wood-beaded seat covers and other car detritus.
Tam had previously worked with nonprofessional performers in his video-art projects (Craigslist-sourced fraternity brothers and cowboys). But recruiting taxi-drivers proved difficult. He tried pitching the idea from back seats, but no luck. Then a black-car driver took him to the “J.F.K. taxi hole.”
“It’s essentially a lot where all the yellow cabs go and take their breaks,” Tam said. Rideshare drivers, too, but they have a separate zone. There’s also a space for prayer. “Ostensibly, they’re there to wait for the call to go to J.F.K., but I met guys that aren’t even drivers anymore, who go to hang out.” Tam and his producer knocked on windows and handed out flyers. Finally, he approached Bilal in the bathroom line. “I did make it clear that this was not necessarily an advocacy project: ‘I’m not an activist, but I do want to tell your story, in a different way.’ ” A way that would eventually involve dancing with metal folding chairs—but one thing at a time.
Outside the museum, Bilal was parked by a fire hydrant in his yellow RAV4. Salah was still en route from Staten Island. Of first meeting Tam, Bilal said, “He asked me, ‘Are you interested in being in a movie?’ ”
“A loose documentary,” Tam clarified.
Bilal was game. “When someone has anger inside them, you want to explore it with the outsider,” he said.
“Did you appreciate then that you would have to dance?” Tam asked.
“It was really interesting, to go for it,” Bilal said. “Falling down, reaching out, maybe looking for some hope. I was sitting in a box where I just felt like, I’m not able to get out of my situation by circling around, making all the moves—this way, that way, left, right, down, up.” He punched a fist into the air.
When things for the brothers turned grim, “we were always leaning on each other,” Bilal said. “Don’t give up. Keep going.” A friend died of a heart attack when the value of his medallion tanked.
“You’re not sick of each other?” Tam asked.
“We fight. But we never really broke up. The next day, it’s as if nothing happened. That’s our attitude.”
Salah called to say that he was stuck on the Verrazzano Bridge.
Tam said that he’d learned to drive in a suburban part of Queens—“and I distinctly remember, for my road test, I didn’t even encounter a traffic light.”
Bilal laughed. “In Lebanon,” he said, “my dad told me, ‘Just drive.’ ”
Salah was still in traffic, so Bilal decided to drive to him. Eventually, he spotted his brother and honked, yelling, “Follow that cab!”
Everyone piled into one car. Salah recounted meeting Tam: “He asked, ‘What kind of song do you like?’ ” The finished video is minimally scored, but, while shooting, Tam had the Elcharfas dance to their favorite tunes. Salah’s: Air Supply’s “Making Love Out of Nothing at All.” Bilal’s: the Scorpions’ “When the Smoke Is Going Down.”
“I’m secretly hoping Mamdani makes an appearance at PS1,” Tam said.
“He’s going to help, but he’s not going to get us back to what was before,” Salah predicted. Both brothers voted for Mamdani, though Bilal also had a laminated photo of Trump (fist raised) dangling from his rearview mirror.
The brothers’ kids recently watched Tam’s film. Bilal: “They were making fun of me—‘You’re getting old! What are you doing?’ ”
Salah: “My daughter, she’s copying me. She starts making the same movements. I said, ‘Maybe I’m going to be a movie star!’ ”
“I explained to them, ‘That’s an art project,’ ” Bilal said. “ ‘The words plus the movement, it makes a story about our situation.’ Hopefully, maybe, we educate the public.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Line of Beauty
literary_pick.author: Alan Hollinghurst
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Booker Prize, this novel is widely regarded as a contemporary classic that captures the essence of 1980s Britain under Margaret Thatcher. Set in the summer of 1983, it follows Nick Guest, a young gay man who becomes entangled in the world of the wealthy Feddens family. Through Nick's increasingly complex relationships and experiences, Hollinghurst explores themes of class, politics, sexuality, and the AIDS crisis, crafting what critics have called 'one of the most brilliant novels ever written' with 'every sentence a work of beauty'.
